{{draft categories| [[Category:onsens]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:秘湯]] [[sn:Secret hot spring]]

{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}} {{afc comment|1=Need to determine how to properly differentiate these four types *{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} *{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=draft}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|12=draft}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt
* {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=draft}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|12=draft}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|12=draft}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|12=draft}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|12=draft}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around {{ill|1975|ja|1975th year|12=draft}}, and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|12=draft}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|12=draft}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|12=draft}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|12=draft}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as {{ill|Arima Hot Spring|ja|有馬温泉|lt=Arima|lt_ja=有馬|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Beppu Hot Spring|ja|別府温泉|lt=Beppu|lt_ja=別府|12=draft}}—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|12=draft}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|12=draft}}, manga artist {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|ja|つげ義春|12=draft}} frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|12=draft}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ({{ill|shower|ja|シャワー|12=draft}}) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|12=draft}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaazumi District|ja|北安曇郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hakuba Village|ja|白馬村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamikawa District|ja|上川郡 (石狩国)|lt=Kamikawa District|lt_ja=上川郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higashikawa Town|ja|東川町|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nihonmatsu City|ja|二本松市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Minamisaku District|ja|南佐久郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nammoku Village|ja|南牧村 (長野県)|lt=Nammoku Village|lt_ja=南牧村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|12=draft}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaazumi District|ja|北安曇郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hakuba Village|ja|白馬村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamikawa District|ja|上川郡 (石狩国)|lt=Kamikawa District|lt_ja=上川郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higashikawa Town|ja|東川町|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nihonmatsu City|ja|二本松市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Minamisaku District|ja|南佐久郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nammoku Village|ja|南牧村 (長野県)|lt=Nammoku Village|lt_ja=南牧村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|12=draft}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Good Mountains, Good Inns, Good Hot Springs: A Mountain Tour Guide for Hitō and Famous Hot Springs – National Edition" (Yama to Keikoku Sha, January 1, 2005) * Edited by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}}, supervised by the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, "Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years", Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association, September 20, 2001
== Related Topics == {{WikiPortalLink|Hot Springs}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hot spring source|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot water spring|ja|熱水泉|1=Hot spring|12=draft}}, {{ill|Geothermal energy|ja|地熱|12=draft}} * {{ill|Geyser|ja|噴気|12=draft}}, {{ill|Man‐made hot spring|ja|造成温泉|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hot spring|ja|温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot spring resort town|ja|温泉街|12=draft}}, {{ill|Public bath|ja|外湯|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot spring ranking|ja|温泉番付|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mixed bathing|ja|混浴|12=draft}} * {{ill|Open‐air bath|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of hot spring resorts in Japan|ja|日本の温泉地一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|Therapeutic bathing|ja|湯治|12=draft}}, {{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Difficult-to-access areas|ja|交通困難地|12=draft}} – many Hitō are designated as such * {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}
== External Links == [https://www.hitou.or.jp/ Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association]

revision.text:
{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}} {{afc comment|1=Need to determine how to properly differentiate these four types *{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} *{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=draft}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|12=draft}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt
* {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=draft}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|12=draft}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|12=draft}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|12=draft}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|12=draft}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around {{ill|1975|ja|1975th year|12=draft}}, and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|12=draft}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|12=draft}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|12=draft}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|12=draft}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as {{ill|Arima Hot Spring|ja|有馬温泉|lt=Arima|lt_ja=有馬|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Beppu Hot Spring|ja|別府温泉|lt=Beppu|lt_ja=別府|12=draft}}—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|12=draft}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|12=draft}}, manga artist {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|ja|つげ義春|12=draft}} frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|12=draft}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ({{ill|shower|ja|シャワー|12=draft}}) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|12=draft}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurobe City|ja|黒部市|12=draft}}) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitaazumi District|ja|北安曇郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hakuba Village|ja|白馬村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamikawa District|ja|上川郡 (石狩国)|lt=Kamikawa District|lt_ja=上川郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higashikawa Town|ja|東川町|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nihonmatsu City|ja|二本松市|12=draft}}) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|12=draft}} ({{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Minamisaku District|ja|南佐久郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nammoku Village|ja|南牧村 (長野県)|lt=Nammoku Village|lt_ja=南牧村|12=draft}}) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|12=draft}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Good Mountains, Good Inns, Good Hot Springs: A Mountain Tour Guide for Hitō and Famous Hot Springs – National Edition" (Yama to Keikoku Sha, January 1, 2005) * Edited by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|12=draft}}, supervised by the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}, "Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years", Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association, September 20, 2001
== Related Topics == {{WikiPortalLink|Hot Springs}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hot spring source|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot water spring|ja|熱水泉|1=Hot spring|12=draft}}, {{ill|Geothermal energy|ja|地熱|12=draft}} * {{ill|Geyser|ja|噴気|12=draft}}, {{ill|Man‐made hot spring|ja|造成温泉|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hot spring|ja|温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot spring resort town|ja|温泉街|12=draft}}, {{ill|Public bath|ja|外湯|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hot spring ranking|ja|温泉番付|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mixed bathing|ja|混浴|12=draft}} * {{ill|Open‐air bath|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of hot spring resorts in Japan|ja|日本の温泉地一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|Therapeutic bathing|ja|湯治|12=draft}}, {{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Difficult-to-access areas|ja|交通困難地|12=draft}} – many Hitō are designated as such * {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年2月}} [[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|登山者しか行けない温泉、[[白馬鑓温泉]]は白馬鑓ヶ岳中腹の標高2,100mに位置する。]] '''秘湯'''（ひとう）は、主に山奥やかつては陸の孤島と云われたような交通の便が悪い場所にあったり、古来為政者や[[修験者]]などの隠し湯など、「ひっそり人知れず、秘境の地に湧出する[[温泉]](湯)」のことを指す<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=秘湯の定義は？有名なのは？ － 温泉大辞典 － BIGLOBE温泉|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE旅行|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=秘湯（ひとう）の意味 - goo国語辞書|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo辞書|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=日本秘湯を守る会について - 日本秘湯を守る会 公式Webサイト|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=日本秘湯を守る会|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=jp}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|和書|title=名誉会長挨拶 - 日本秘湯を守る会 公式Webサイト|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=日本秘湯を守る会|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=jp}}</ref>。
「秘湯」とは[[1975年]]ごろに作られた[[造語]]で、考案者は[[日本秘湯を守る会]]の設立を提唱した[[岩木一二三]]である(昭和2年生まれ、平成13年没)<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" />。彼は、1965年(昭和40年)に株式会社朝日旅行会を創業<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal|author=|year=2005年1月1日|title=「日本秘湯を守る会」の30年|journal=旅の手帖|volume=第29巻1号|page=44-45}}</ref>。昭和40年代半ばは、温泉イコール歓楽地が全盛の時代。旅を味わうものとは程遠かった。その頃、彼は戦後の引き揚げ船からみた故国「国破れて山河あり」の万感の郷愁を胸に秘め、一人になって人間を見つめ直したい、そんな想いで山の中の静かな温泉宿を求め全国をめぐり歩いた。そうした旅の実体験と想いをきっかけに、不便な山を歩く旅と素朴な山の温泉宿を通して旅本来の姿を知ってもらおうと、1972年(昭和47年)に「秘湯を歩く会」を創ったのがそもそもの始まり。 山歩きしなくては辿りつけない奥地や[[陸の孤島]]だったような交通の便の悪い場所にある温泉宿、山奥の[[湯治場]]、豪雪などで半年しか営業できない山の温泉、修験者やかつての藩主の隠し湯など、「ひっそりとして素朴で人に知られていない、秘められてきたような、秘境に湧出する[[温泉]](湯)」を略して命名したものである<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の秘湯|date=1977年～2000年|year=|publisher=朝日旅行会|pages=|author=日本秘湯を守る会監修|edition=第1版～第13版}}</ref>。彼が「秘湯」という言葉に込めた深淵な想いは、日本秘湯を守る会のガイドブック『日本の秘湯』の序文「秘湯をさがして」の中に今も遺されている<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の秘湯|date=2009年2月|year=|publisher=(株)朝日旅行|pages=2|edition=21版|author=一般社団法人日本秘湯を守る会監修}}</ref>。
== 概要 == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|北海道の原生林の中の渓流沿いにぽつりと露天風呂が点在する[[鹿の湯]]]] 古くから山奥のひっそりとした温泉を求める温泉愛好家は数多く存在したが、その傾向が大衆的に強まっていったのは戦後[[高度成長期]]以降である。[[温泉宿]]が社員旅行の旅行先として使われるようになり、[[有馬温泉|有馬]]、[[熱海]]、[[別府温泉|別府]]などに代表されるような大温泉地が[[歓楽地]]化するにつれ、素朴な湯治場の雰囲気を温泉を求める温泉ファンは喧騒から離れた僻地や小規模な温泉へ足を運ぶようになった。秘湯という言葉も、この頃から用いられはじめたとされる。
この当時（[[1960年代]]）、漫画家である[[つげ義春]]がしきりに僻地の鄙びた温泉地、[[湯治場]]へ足を運び当時の温泉や湯治場の雰囲気を多くの作品や旅の絵などに刻印している<ref name="asahi">アサヒグラフ1959年2月14日号</ref>。また、多くの秘湯ファンにつげの及ぼした影響は大きい。
[[1980年代]]に入ると「秘湯」ブームとなり、巨大な温泉宿や歓楽街を有した温泉地よりも山奥の素朴な一軒宿を好む傾向が強まる。ブームにより秘湯の大衆化が進展し、素朴さが特徴であった宿にも快適装備（浴室の[[シャワー]]や、[[水洗トイレ]]など）が設けられるようになり俗化が進んだ。現時点では、長距離歩かないと本来の意味での秘湯に辿りつくことは容易ではない。
== 日本の主な秘湯 == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|標高2,100m。徒歩約13時間の[[高天原温泉]]（[[富山県]][[富山市]]）。]] 何を以って「秘湯」であるとするかは議論の余地が残されているものの、アクセスが困難な秘境らしい環境の温泉を試みに列挙する。
*[[高天原温泉]]（[[富山県]][[富山市]]）標高2,100m。徒歩約13時間。 *[[仙人温泉]]（富山県[[黒部市]]）標高1,550m。徒歩約8時間。 *[[阿曽原温泉]]（富山県[[黒部市]]）標高900m。徒歩約5時間。 *[[白馬鑓温泉]]（[[長野県]][[北安曇郡]][[白馬村]]）標高2,100m。徒歩4～5時間。 *[[赤岳鉱泉]]（長野県[[茅野市]]）標高2,240m。徒歩約3時間。 *[[中岳温泉]]（[[北海道]][[上川郡 (石狩国)|上川郡]][[東川町]]）標高1,840m。徒歩約2時間。 *[[くろがね温泉]]（[[福島県]][[二本松市]]）標高1,400m。徒歩約2時間。 *[[本沢温泉]]（長野県[[南佐久郡]][[南牧村 (長野県)|南牧村]]）標高2,150m。徒歩約2時間。 いずれも登山装備を要する。
== 「秘湯」の宿 == 「秘湯」を名乗る上での規制はないため、「秘湯」は自称することが可能である。
もともとは、造語「秘湯」の産みの親である[[岩木一二三]]が創業した[[朝日旅行|株式会社朝日旅行]]（旧・朝日旅行会;2020年3月廃業）の内部団体であった「[[日本秘湯を守る会]](現:一般社団法人)」が、秘湯ブームの源となっている<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|和書|title=秘湯を守って 二十五周年のあしあと|date=2001年9月20日|year=|publisher=日本秘湯を守る会|pages=16-22,71|editor=岩木一二三}}</ref>。
[[日本秘湯を守る会]]では、宿が入会申請し、審査や視察が行われる。会として独自の「秘湯」その他の基準や要件を満たし審査を通れば、加盟する秘湯の宿として承認される。かつては、自然も豊かで開発の手も届かない辺境の田舎の湯治場だった宿や、除雪できないほどの豪雪地や道路などが冬季封鎖され半年しか営業できない山の温泉宿などが、ほとんどだった。国立公園や国定公園、自然公園などに立地していたり、登山基地となって命の砦になる温泉宿も多い<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=地熱発電の隠された真実|date=2012年10月|year=|publisher=日本秘湯を守る宿|pages=|author=佐藤好億監修}}</ref>。しかし、秘湯らしかったところが、時代とともに改築や自動車道路の開通等により往時の秘湯のイメージが薄れてしまったところもある。しかし、温泉や自然を保全し、秘湯の文化や歴史を守っていく後継手としてかつ又その探求者としての意義もあり、会員資格はそのまま継続になっている<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の秘湯|date=2009年2月|year=|publisher=(株)朝日旅行|pages=194-195|author=一般社団法人日本秘湯を守る会監修|edition=第21版}}</ref>。
1975年(昭和50年)当時は、団体ツアーや娯楽観光ブームの時流に乗り遅れた感のある「ひなびた(寂れた)温泉＝秘湯」と言われることを、温泉旅館自身が好まない風潮だった。そんな中、1982年(昭和57年)に日本秘湯を守る会の冊子『日本の秘湯(第3版)』がテレビ朝日の目に留まり、昼間の番組に秘湯が取りあげられ、次々とテレビを中心に秘湯ブームが起こった<ref name=":4" />。そうして「秘湯」という言葉が浸透するにつれて、一般的なイメージが形造られ、旅人もより「秘湯」らしさを探求するようになった。「秘湯」が旅先になる時代になり、温泉旅館も「秘湯」を好んで名乗るようなった。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *「いい山 いい宿 いい温泉 秘湯・名湯めぐりの山旅ガイド 全国版」(株)山と渓谷社、2005年1月1日 * 岩木一二三編集・日本秘湯を守る会監修「秘湯を守って 二十五周年のあしあと」日本秘湯を守る会、2001年9月20日
== 関連項目 == {{ウィキポータルリンク|温泉}} * [[野湯]] * [[源泉]]、[[熱水泉]]、[[地熱]] * [[噴気]]、[[造成温泉]] * [[温泉]]、[[温泉街]]、[[外湯]]、[[温泉番付]]、[[混浴]] * [[露天風呂]] * [[日本の温泉地一覧]] * [[湯治]]、[[湯治場]] * [[隠し湯]]、[[修験道]] * [[交通困難地]] - 多くの秘湯が指定されている * [[日本秘湯を守る会]]
== 外部リンク == [https://www.hitou.or.jp/ 日本秘湯を守る会]
{{Onsen-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ひとう}} [[Category:温泉]]

もともとは、造語「秘湯」の産みの親である[[岩木一二三]]が創業した[[朝日旅行|株式会社朝日旅行]]（旧・朝日旅行会;2020年3月廃業）の内部団体であった「[[日本秘湯を守る会]](現:一般社団法人)」が、秘湯ブームの源となっている<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|和書|title=秘湯を守って 二十五周年のあしあと|date=2001年9月20日|year=|publisher=日本秘湯を守る会|pages=16-22,71|editor=岩木一二三}}</ref>。
[[日本秘湯を守る会]]では、宿が入会申請し、審査や視察が行われる。会として独自の「秘湯」その他の基準や要件を満たし審査を通れば、加盟する秘湯の宿として承認される。かつては、自然も豊かで開発の手も届かない辺境の田舎の湯治場だった宿や、除雪できないほどの豪雪地や道路などが冬季封鎖され半年しか営業できない山の温泉宿などが、ほとんどだった。国立公園や国定公園、自然公園などに立地していたり、登山基地となって命の砦になる温泉宿も多い<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=地熱発電の隠された真実|date=2012年10月|year=|publisher=日本秘湯を守る宿|pages=|author=佐藤好億監修}}</ref>。しかし、秘湯らしかったところが、時代とともに改築や自動車道路の開通等により往時の秘湯のイメージが薄れてしまったところもある。しかし、温泉や自然を保全し、秘湯の文化や歴史を守っていく後継手としてかつ又その探求者としての意義もあり、会員資格はそのまま継続になっている<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|和書|title=日本の秘湯|date=2009年2月|year=|publisher=(株)朝日旅行|pages=194-195|author=一般社団法人日本秘湯を守る会監修|edition=第21版}}</ref>。
1975年(昭和50年)当時は、団体ツアーや娯楽観光ブームの時流に乗り遅れた感のある「ひなびた(寂れた)温泉＝秘湯」と言われることを、温泉旅館自身が好まない風潮だった。そんな中、1982年(昭和57年)に日本秘湯を守る会の冊子『日本の秘湯(第3版)』がテレビ朝日の目に留まり、昼間の番組に秘湯が取りあげられ、次々とテレビを中心に秘湯ブームが起こった<ref name=":4" />。そうして「秘湯」という言葉が浸透するにつれて、一般的なイメージが形造られ、旅人もより「秘湯」らしさを探求するようになった。「秘湯」が旅先になる時代になり、温泉旅館も「秘湯」を好んで名乗るようなった。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *「いい山 いい宿 いい温泉 秘湯・名湯めぐりの山旅ガイド 全国版」(株)山と渓谷社、2005年1月1日 * 岩木一二三編集・日本秘湯を守る会監修「秘湯を守って 二十五周年のあしあと」日本秘湯を守る会、2001年9月20日
== 関連項目 == {{ウィキポータルリンク|温泉}} * [[野湯]] * [[源泉]]、[[熱水泉]]、[[地熱]] * [[噴気]]、[[造成温泉]] * [[温泉]]、[[温泉街]]、[[外湯]]、[[温泉番付]]、[[混浴]] * [[露天風呂]] * [[日本の温泉地一覧]] * [[湯治]]、[[湯治場]] * [[隠し湯]]、[[修験道]] * [[交通困難地]] - 多くの秘湯が指定されている * [[日本秘湯を守る会]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to determine how to properly differentiate these four types *{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|1=Wilderness hot springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wilderness hot springs|qq=}} {{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt
* {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wilderness hot springs|qq=}}
{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Good Mountains, Good Inns, Good Hot Springs: A Mountain Tour Guide for Hitō and Famous Hot Springs – National Edition" (Yama to Keikoku Sha, January 1, 2005) * Edited by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}}, supervised by the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, "Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years", Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association, September 20, 2001

*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

{{afc comment|1=Need to determine how to properly differentiate these four types *{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|1=Wilderness hot springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wilderness hot springs|qq=}} {{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt
* {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wilderness hot springs|qq=}}
{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
