*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2309838}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=|1=Shikanoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11676461}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=|1=onsen ryokan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562938}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=|1=Atami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11569072}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水洗式便所|1=flush toilet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q268534}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=|1=Azohara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657451}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=|1=Akadake Kōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108808349}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=|1=Kurogane Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216264}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Honzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520497}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Good Mountains, Good Inns, Good Hot Springs: A Mountain Tour Guide for Hitō and Famous Hot Springs – National Edition" (Yama to Keikoku Sha, January 1, 2005) * Edited by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, supervised by the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, "Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years", Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association, September 20, 2001

{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2309838}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=|1=Shikanoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11676461}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=|1=onsen ryokan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562938}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=|1=Atami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11569072}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水洗式便所|1=flush toilet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q268534}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=|1=Azohara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657451}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=|1=Akadake Kōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108808349}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=|1=Kurogane Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216264}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Honzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520497}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}

In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=|1=Shikanoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11676461}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=|1=onsen ryokan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562938}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=|1=Atami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11569072}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水洗式便所|1=flush toilet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q268534}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=|1=Azohara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657451}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=|1=Akadake Kōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108808349}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=|1=Kurogane Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216264}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Honzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520497}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2309838}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=|1=Shikanoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11676461}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=|1=onsen ryokan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562938}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=|1=Atami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11569072}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水洗式便所|1=flush toilet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q268534}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.
== Major Hitō in Japan == [[File:Takamagahara Hot Spring.jpg|thumb|right|220px|At an elevation of 2,100 m. Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:
* {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 13 hours on foot. * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|qq=|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 1,550 m; approximately 8 hours on foot. * {{ill|Asohara Onsen|ja|阿曽原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asohara Onsen|qq=|1=Azohara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657451}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurobe, Toyama|Kurobe City]]) – elevation 900 m; approximately 5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitaazumi District, Nagano|Kitaazumi District]], [[:en:Hakuba|Hakuba Village]]) – elevation 2,100 m; approximately 4–5 hours on foot. * {{ill|Akadake Mineral Spring|ja|赤岳鉱泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akadake Mineral Spring|qq=|1=Akadake Kōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108808349}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]]) – elevation 2,240 m; approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=|1=Kurogane Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216264}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Honzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520497}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.

{{unreferenced|date=February 2012}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} *{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:51, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} (Wilderness Hot Springs): These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.
* {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} (Secret Hot Springs): Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.
* {{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}} (Hidden Hot Springs): Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.
* {{ill|Drawn‑off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‑off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}} (Drawn‑off Hot Springs): Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.
Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:白馬鑓温泉露天風呂Img091.jpg|thumb|right|220px|A hot spring accessible only to mountaineers, {{ill|Hakuba Yari Onsen|ja|白馬鑓温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuba Yari Onsen|qq=|1=Hakuba Yari Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11580484}} is located on the mid-slope of Mount Hakuba Yari at an elevation of 2,100 m.]]
'''Hitō''' (秘湯) refers to hot springs that are typically located deep in the mountains or in areas with poor access that were once described as {{ill|isolated island|ja|陸の孤島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/isolated island|qq=}}, and which include secret springs used by ancient rulers or {{ill|Shugendō practitioner|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō practitioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2309838}}—that is, hot springs that quietly emerge in hidden, remote regions.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=What is the definition of Hitō? Famous examples? - Hot Spring Encyclopedia - BIGLOBE Onsen|url=https://travel.biglobe.ne.jp/onsen/jiten/ninki_07.html|website=BIGLOBE Travel|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=The meaning of Hitō (秘湯) - goo Dictionary|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E7%A7%98%E6%B9%AF/|website=goo Dictionary|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=About the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association - Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|Japanese source|title=Greeting from the Honorary President - Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association Official Website|url=https://www.hitou.or.jp/|website=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|accessdate=2021-02-01|language=ja}}</ref>.
"Hitō" was a coined term created around [[:en:1975|1975]], and its originator was the proponent of establishing the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (born in Showa 2, died in Heisei 13). He founded {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|株式会社朝日旅行会|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1965 (Showa 40). In the mid‑Showa 40s, when hot springs were equated with entertainment districts and far removed from the true spirit of travel, he carried a deep nostalgia—evoked by the sight of repatriation ships after the war and the notion of “a country in ruins but with enduring mountains and rivers”—and set out across the nation in search of quiet mountain hot spring inns where one might reflect on life in solitude. Inspired by these experiences and sentiments, he established the “Hitō Hiking Association” in 1972 (Showa 47) to help people rediscover the true essence of travel through journeys on rugged mountain paths and visits to humble hot spring inns.
== Overview == [[File:鹿の湯（しかのゆ）露天風呂（大）P6270560.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Open-air baths are scattered along a mountain stream in the primeval forests of Hokkaido at {{ill|Shikanoyu|ja|鹿の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikanoyu|qq=|1=Shikanoyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11676461}}]] Although many hot spring enthusiasts have long sought secluded onsens deep in the mountains, this trend became more widespread after the {{ill|Japanese post-war economic miracle|ja|高度成長期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese post-war economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}. As {{ill|hot spring inns|ja|温泉宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring inns|qq=|1=onsen ryokan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562938}} began to be used for company outings, and major hot spring resorts—such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima]], {{ill|Atami|ja|熱海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami|qq=|1=Atami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11569072}}, and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu]]—transformed into entertainment districts ({{ill|entertainment district|ja|歓楽地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/entertainment district|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), hot spring enthusiasts in search of the simple, therapeutic atmosphere of traditional bathing resorts increasingly turned to remote or smaller onsens. It is said that the term “Hitō” began to be used around this time.
During the {{ill|1960s|ja|1960th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1960s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, manga artist [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]] frequently visited rustic, offbeat therapeutic hot spring resorts ({{ill|Therapeutic hot spring resort|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Therapeutic hot spring resort|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}), capturing their unique ambiance in many works and travel sketches.<ref name="asahi">Asahi Graph, February 14, 1959</ref> Moreover, Tsuge’s influence on many Hitō enthusiasts was considerable.
In the {{ill|1980s|ja|1980th year 代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1980s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a Hitō boom emerged, as more people favored humble, single‐room mountain inns over large resorts with entertainment districts. This trend led to the popularization of Hitō; even the once‑rustic inns began incorporating modern amenities (such as showers ([[:en:Shower|shower]]) and flush toilets ({{ill|flush toilet|ja|水洗トイレ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flush toilet|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水洗式便所|1=flush toilet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q268534}})), thus becoming somewhat commercialized. Today, reaching an authentic Hitō often requires a long, arduous hike.

Approximately 13 hours on foot to reach {{ill|Takamagahara Hot Spring|ja|高天原温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamagahara Hot Spring|qq=|1=Takamagahara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669112}} ([[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]]).]] Although what exactly qualifies as “Hitō” remains open to debate, here is a tentative list of hot springs set in remote, almost otherworldly locations:

approximately 3 hours on foot. * {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} ([[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Kamikawa (Ishikari) District, Hokkaido|Kamikawa District]], [[:en:Higashikawa, Hokkaido|Higashikawa Town]]) – elevation 1,840 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Kurogane Onsen|ja|くろがね温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurogane Onsen|qq=|1=Kurogane Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216264}} ([[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu City]]) – elevation 1,400 m; approximately 2 hours on foot. * {{ill|Honzawa Onsen|ja|本沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Honzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520497}} ([[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku District]], [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Nammoku Village]]) – elevation 2,150 m; approximately 2 hours on foot.
All of these locations require mountaineering equipment.
== 'Hitō' Inns == There are no regulations governing the use of the term “Hitō,” so any inn may claim it.
Originally, the internal group of {{ill|Asahi Travel|ja|朝日旅行|lt=Asahi Travel Company|lt_ja=株式会社朝日旅行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Travel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (formerly Asahi Travel Association; ceased operations in March 2020), founded by {{ill|Iwakki Hifumi|ja|岩木一二三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakki Hifumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the originator of the coined term “Hitō,” formed the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}} (currently a general incorporated association), which sparked the Hitō boom.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Preserving Hitō: The Footprints of 25 Years|date=September 20, 2001|publisher=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|pages=16–22, 71|editor=Iwakki Hifumi}}</ref>
Within the {{ill|Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|ja|日本秘湯を守る会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|qq=|1=Japan Association of Secluded Hot Spring Inns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11508755}}, inns must apply for membership and undergo evaluations and inspections. If an inn meets the association’s unique criteria and requirements for being a Hitō, it is approved as a member. In the past, most such inns were rustic therapeutic resorts located in remote rural areas abundant in nature and beyond the reach of development—or mountain onsens that could operate only half the year due to heavy snowfall rendering roads impassable. Many are also situated in national, quasi‑national, or nature parks, serving as mountaineering bases and bastions of life.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=The Hidden Truth of Geothermal Power Generation|date=October 2012|publisher=Japan Hitō Preservation Inns|author=Supervisor Sato Koyoshi}}</ref> However, as renovations and the opening of roads have diminished some of the original character of Hitō, the membership system remains in place due to its significance in preserving hot springs, nature, and the cultural history of Hitō.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|Japanese source|title=Japan's Hitō|date=February 2009|publisher=Asahi Travel Company|pages=194–195|author=Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association|edition=21st edition}}</ref>
In 1975 (Showa 50), there was a prevailing sentiment among hot spring inns against being labeled as “rustic (declining) onsens = Hitō,” since that term implied they had fallen behind the trends of group tours and the leisure travel boom. Yet in 1982 (Showa 57), a booklet by the Japan Secret Hot Springs Preservation Association titled “Japan’s Hitō (3rd edition)” caught the attention of TV Asahi; after being featured on a daytime program, a Hitō boom swept across television. As the term “Hitō” became more widely known, its common image was shaped, and travelers began to actively seek out the true essence of Hitō. It became an era when Hitō itself was a sought‐after travel destination, and hot spring inns began to proudly adopt the label.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
